title: Can This Trust Be Allowed to Go in Vain?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; election, court, price and crime-report details have been retained with their source attribution; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 13 February 2015, a by-election will be held in Srirangam. Why? The person elected by that constituency and seated as Chief Minister was sentenced by a Special Court to four years' imprisonment and fined Rs 100 crore in the disproportionate-assets case. Her office as Chief Minister and even her qualification as Srirangam MLA fell vacant, forcing this by-election upon the constituency. The very punishment imposed on its former representative by the Special Court provides sufficient reason for the people not to vote for that party again and be deceived.

Newspapers report that a letter written by Jayalalithaa seeking support for the AIADMK candidate is being distributed in the constituency. She calls it “a by-election caused by the judgment in the case filed against me” and “a by-election produced by the dance of conspiracy and fate acting together.” The courts must take note that she has thus criticised their judgment as a conspiracy. Her punishment was neither fate nor conspiracy. It was something she sought out through her own conduct.

Every day, I learn how constructively and enthusiastically our Udanpirappus are working in Srirangam. Were not our comrades praised even by the elder statesman Bhaktavatsalam as “DMK workers who can drink a single tea and carry on election work”? I am happy to learn that they are working with that same energy in this by-election.

Because it concerns one constituency and because of my health, the General Secretary, our esteemed Professor, and Treasurer younger brother M.K. Stalin insisted that I not come in person to campaign. They stopped me, saying I should not strain myself. Though I am not there, you know well that my thoughts remain wholly with this election.

Our General Secretary is visiting. Treasurer Stalin personally launched the campaign and continues to inspect and accelerate it. Principal Secretary younger brother Duraimurugan is there. Newly appointed Deputy General Secretary Dindigul I. Periyasamy is campaigning. The Women's Wing, led by secretary and poet Kanimozhi MP, will campaign, as will members of the movement's other wings and artists from the film world. Stalin will return for the final stage.

Campaign committee chair younger brother K.N. Nehru works day and night, spinning like a top, and ensures that his team works well too. Experienced district secretaries and those newly appointed are staying in Srirangam, visiting homes and seeking votes. Reports continue to reach me.

News reports say ruling-party Ministers have descended upon the constituency and are scattering money accumulated by improper means. The DMK submitted a complaint with photographic evidence to the Election Commission about a huge kitchen and dining hall established by the AIADMK in a coconut grove at Pettaivaithalai, where biryani and other food were allegedly served three times a day to thousands of voters, while police took no action despite complaints.

Chief Electoral Officer Sandeep Saxena reportedly directed the Tiruchi Collector to inspect the location and said that feeding voters would be an offence for which a case would be registered. But the Collector assumed office only days before the election announcement and had previously served in Theni, the home district of present Chief Minister Panneerselvam.

Newspapers say the Secretariat has ground to a halt because all Ministers are camped in Srirangam. On Anna Memorial Day, no Minister other than the Chief Minister visited Anna's memorial. The annual communal meal in temples was not held. What tricks will the ruling party and its Ministers attempt during the remaining five or six days?

Government officials under those campaigning Ministers are acting in support of the ruling-party candidate. When the Election Commission failed to act on the DMK's request to remove bogus voters, advocates Girirajan, Neelakandan and Paranthaman filed a High Court case for our candidate, younger brother Anand.

Before Chief Justice Kaul and Justice Sundaresan, our advocate younger brother Wilson argued: “The electoral roll contains large numbers of bogus entries. The names of dead people, fictitious voters and the same voter appearing twice are widespread. The bogus entries must be removed before the election.” The First Bench ordered removal of bogus voters and publication of a corrected supplementary roll two days before polling. DMK advocates Devaraj, Muthukumar, Arun and Anitha appeared in the case.

Should this Government not be warned and instructed over its continuing anti-democratic excesses? That is why this by-election matters. If the ruling party wins again, it will imagine no one can oppose it and indulge in still more anti-people and anti-democratic acts with arrogance.

What are the AIADMK Government's achievements? Electricity tariffs have risen, but no one knows when electricity will arrive. Since taking office, it has repeatedly boasted that power cuts will end, yet reliable supply remains unavailable. Factories are closing. Nokia has gone, Foxconn has gone, and Tamil Nadu has no Government concerned about them.

Milk prices have risen, while corruption has engulfed Aavin. The ruling party itself removed the responsible Minister. Does that not mean the AIADMK admitted the scale of corruption? Bus fares have risen.

Prices have climbed. Toor dal rose from Rs 85 a kilogram last month to Rs 95; urad dal from Rs 80 to Rs 90; moong dal from Rs 105 to Rs 115; and chana dal from Rs 45 to Rs 55. Hill garlic rose from Rs 120 to Rs 150, and country garlic from Rs 90 to Rs 130.

One need not even ask about cement. They announced “Amma Cement.” In Coimbatore, a bag sold for Rs 330 to Rs 340 last November. It rose to Rs 370 on 9 December, Rs 375 on the twelfth, Rs 380 on the twenty-sixth, Rs 385 on 3 January, and, after another Rs 10 increase, Rs 395 two days ago. Retail shops charge Rs 400.
Karnataka revenue officials seized 258 lorries at Attibele yesterday for carrying sand from Tamil Nadu without permission. What Tamil Nadu officials ignored was exposed in Karnataka.
The eleven-storey building at Moulivakkam collapsed, killing more than sixty people. Two days ago, ten workers drowned in effluent beside a SIPCOT factory near Ranipet.

When the former Chief Minister held office, she read some statement under Rule 110 every day so that an announcement appeared in her name in the newspapers. What became of those schemes? When questioned in the Assembly, present Chief Minister Panneerselvam answered only about a few announcements and said nothing of the rest.

Law and order has become a laughing stock at every street corner. Under AIADMK rule, no day passes without chain-snatching, robbery, murder or theft. Looking at reports from this day alone, *Dinamalar* boxed and listed “Yesterday's chain-snatching tally.” It also reported “Seventy mobile phones, a sack of rice and ten pairs of trousers stolen.”

*Dinamani* listed “Seven shops broken into.” *Dina Thanthi* compiled eighteen incidents in three columns under “Young woman alone at home near Selaiyur tied up; ten sovereigns stolen.” The paper also reported a trader drugged and robbed of Rs 9.5 lakh, donation boxes broken at four temples, shops burgled in Ayanavaram, and jewellery snatched from a woman engineer in Chromepet. These proclaim the AIADMK's governing ability.

Yesterday morning, robbers pushed down an IAS officer walking on the beach road and snatched his mobile phone. Forty-two-year-old Annalatha was attacked by intruders at her home in Avadi's Thirumullaivoyal, fell into a coma and died last night without regaining consciousness. A lawyer was murdered inside the Egmore court complex days ago. Near Gummidipoondi yesterday, Thayalakshmi and her daughter Nirosha were hacked to death as they slept. Tamil Nadu is becoming an unsafe land under this AIADMK Government.

No one knows what the new Chief Minister is doing. Poor man, even after 125 days in office he still cannot enter the Chief Minister's chamber and remains in his old Finance Minister's room. This Government's own calendar cannot properly display his photograph as Chief Minister. Every announcement appears in the name of “People's Chief Minister Amma.”

Every vehicle at the Republic Day parade displayed a large photograph of the convicted former Chief Minister; not one showed the present Chief Minister. A photograph showed him going alone to pay tribute on Anna Memorial Day without a single Minister accompanying him. Tamil Nadu looks like a State without a Chief Minister.
Weekly magazines have written page after page criticising him and this Government, but ruling-party members pay no attention and feel neither concern nor shame.

Should they not be taught a democratic lesson? The Srirangam by-election offers that good opportunity. I appeal to the voters of Srirangam: teach the ruling AIADMK a fitting lesson on the thirteenth, give it an opportunity to reform and show concern for the people at least during its remaining days, and vote for DMK candidate younger brother Anand under the Rising Sun symbol.
Give us the news: “Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam wins the Srirangam by-election.” I trust that you certainly will. Can that trust be allowed to go in vain?
With affection,
M.K.
5 February 2015
